sections.summary: Records 2023 Codex amendments: changes to Section 6.2 (labelling of non‑retail containers) and addition of Section 7 on food additives.

Defines scope: applies to commercial varieties/types of culantro coyote (Eryngyum foetidum) supplied to consumers in bundles of fresh leaves or equivalent presentations; culantro for industrial processing is excluded.

Sets out quality provisions for culantro coyote including minimum requirements (sound, clean, pest‑free, no foreign odours/taste, no temperature damage, fresh appearance), requirement to withstand transport and arrive satisfactorily, and classification into three classes (Extra, Class I, Class II) with class‑specific defect tolerances.

Lists minimum requirements for all classes: produce must be sound and free from rotting/deterioration, practically free of visible foreign matter and pests, free of foreign smell/taste and temperature damage, and present a fresh appearance with natural colouring.

sections.section: 2.1.1 The development and condition of culantro coyote must be such as to enable it:
sections.summary: Requires culantro coyote to be developed and conditioned so it can withstand transport and handling and arrive in satisfactory condition at the place of destination.
States that culantro coyote is classified into three classes: Extra, Class I and Class II.

Extra class: bundles must be of superior quality, characteristic of the variety/type, and free of defects except very slight superficial ones that do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation.

Class I: bundles must be of good quality and characteristic of the variety/type, allowing slight defects provided they do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation — blemishes up to 5% of leaves, broken leaves up to 10%, split leaves up to 10%.

Class II: includes bundles not qualifying for higher classes but meeting minimum requirements; allows blemishes up to 15%, broken leaves up to 20%, and split leaves up to 20%, provided essential characteristics, keeping quality and presentation are retained.

Defines sizing by number of bundles per kilogram and provides a size code table linking weight per bundle to bundles per kg (Size 1: >200 g, <5/kg; 2: 150–200 g, 5–7/kg; 3: 100–149 g, 8–10/kg; 4: 50–99 g, 11–20/kg; 5: <50 g, >20/kg).

sections.section: Size specifications for bundles of leaves
sections.summary: Provides size specifications for bundles of leaves via a table mapping bundle weight ranges to the corresponding number of bundles per kilogram for sizes 1–5.

Sets tolerances allowed in each package: class‑specific quality tolerances (Extra: 5% allowed to meet Class I; Class I: 10% allowed to meet Class II; Class II: 10% may fail class/minimum except rotten/unfit) and size tolerances (10% may be the adjacent size above/below).
Heading only — introduces the quality tolerances subsection (details provided in following subclauses).
Extra class tolerance: up to 5% by number or weight of bundles in a package may not meet Extra requirements but may meet Class I or fall within Class I tolerances.
Class I tolerance: up to 10% by number or weight of bundles in a package may not meet Class I requirements but may meet Class II or fall within Class II tolerances.
Class II tolerance: up to 10% by number or weight of bundles may fail to meet Class II or the minimum requirements, excluding produce affected by rotting or otherwise unfit for consumption.
Size tolerances: for all classes and presentations, up to 10% by number or weight of bundles may correspond to the size immediately above and/or below that indicated on the package.
Heading only — introduces presentation provisions (details provided in subsequent subsections).

Uniformity requirement: each package must contain bundles uniform in origin, variety/commercial type, quality, size and colour, and the visible portion of the contents must be representative of the entire package.

Requires bundles to be packed to protect the produce using new, clean materials that do not cause external or internal damage (printing/labels may use non‑toxic ink/glue) and mandates packing in containers in accordance with the international Code of Practice for Packaging and Transport of Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 44-1995).

Containers for culantro coyote must meet quality, hygiene, ventilation and resistance requirements to ensure proper handling, shipping and preservation, and packages must be free of foreign matter and odors.
Non‑substantive heading introducing permitted forms of presentation for bundles (no specific requirements in this section).
States that, when presented 'individually', each bundle must be packed in a separate bag.
States that, when presented 'in bunches', several bundles are placed in each bag according to their size.
Non‑substantive heading for marking and labelling requirements (no provisions in this section text).

Introductory provision that consumer package labelling must meet the General Standard for the Labelling of Pre‑packaged Foods (CXS 119-85) and that the standard includes additional specific provisions.

If the produce is not visible from the outside, consumer packages must be labelled with the name 'culantro coyote' (or the local marketing name) and may include variety/commercial type and the form of presentation (individual or in bunches).
Requires labelling of non‑retail containers to follow the General Standard for the Labelling of Non‑Retail Containers of Foods (CXS 346-2021).
For non‑retail containers, if contents are not visible the package must be labelled with the name 'culantro coyote' (or local marketing name); naming the variety or commercial type is optional.
States that no food additives are permitted in foods conforming to this standard.
Obliges the produce to comply with maximum levels in the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995) and with Codex maximum residue limits for pesticides.

Recommends preparation and handling in accordance with the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969), the Code of Hygienic Practice for Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 53-2003) and other relevant Codex texts, and requires compliance with any microbiological criteria established per CXG 21-1997.
